title: Frequency locking in lasing ZnO nanowire pairs
authors: Ann-Kathrin Kollak, Lukas R. Jäger, Hark Hoe Tan, Carsten Ronning
abstract: Frequency locking between coupled laser systems provides a powerful mechanism for stabilizing and controlling coherent emission, yet its implementation and applicability down to the nanoscale remains unknown and unexplored. Here, we demonstrate optical coupling and frequency locking in closely spaced ZnO nanowire lasers operating in the extreme near field (gap < 10 nm). We observe both full and partial frequency locking, manifested as the alignment of all or a subset of the lasing modes, by spatially controlling the optical excitation. We also observe single-mode lasing in a coupled nanowire pair where the multi-mode lasing of individual nanowires is suppressed. In contrast to previously reported coupled-cavity nanowire lasers, where spectral control arises from static filtering mechanisms such as the Vernier effect, our results indicate a dynamically established relationship between actively lasing nanowires. These findings establish frequency locking as a robust and tunable mechanism in nanowire lasers, opening new routes toward stabilized and controllable nanoscale light sources for integrated nanophotonic systems.
summary: The researchers demonstrated that two ZnO nanowire lasers can be dynamically synchronized through frequency locking when placed extremely close together. By controlling where the laser pump hits the wires, they can switch between full, partial, and no locking. This provides a robust way to control light at the nanoscale for future integrated photonic circuits.
main_problem: Investigating the implementation and control of frequency locking and optical coupling in ZnO nanowire lasers at the nanoscale, specifically distinguishing between dynamic nonlinear interaction and static spectral filtering.
main_result: Demonstrated that closely spaced ZnO nanowire lasers (gap < 10 nm) exhibit dynamic frequency locking, including full and partial locking regimes, which can be actively controlled via spatial excitation distribution.
method: Micromanipulation of ZnO nanowires into pairs, followed by spatially resolved micro-photoluminescence spectroscopy using an inhomogeneous optical pump spot.
model_or_system: A pair of ZnO (Zinc Oxide) nanowires assembled in a staggered geometry on a SiO2/Si substrate, operating as evanescently coupled Fabry-Perot resonators in the extreme near-field.
key_observables: Lasing photoluminescence spectra, Free Spectral Range (FSR), group index (ng), spatio-spectral intensity maps, and side-mode suppression ratio (SMSR).
important_parameters: Gap distance (< 10 nm), pump power, detuning (cavity length difference), and pumping asymmetry (relative excitation intensity).
main_assumptions: The threshold for lasing is operationally defined by the emergence of Fabry-Perot modes; the group index is approximated as a linear function of wavelength.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the nanowire assembly schematic, individual spectra before assembly, SEM images of the pair, and spatially resolved spectra. Supplementary figures show pump spot intensity maps, single nanowire spectral stability, and the derivation of the group index.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of nanoscale coupling, describes the experimental assembly and spectroscopic setup, presents the demonstration of frequency locking and its tunability, and concludes with the implications for nanophotonic systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: a Schematic depiction of the nanowire pair preparation. b Lasing photoluminescence spectra of individual nanowires 1 and 2 (before pair assembly). c SEM image of nanowire pair 1-2. Dashed lines indicate which emission track in the CCD image originates from which end facet. d Spatially resolved lasing spectra of nanowire pair 1-2 excited at position 1 in the inhomogeneous pump spot. Both NWs emit peaks at identical spectral positions. The gray lines mark the spectral positions of the lasing peaks emitted by NW 1 (dotted) and NW 2 (dashed) without coupling. The inset in the lower panel shows the camera image of the pump spot; the white line indicates the nanowire-pair–pump-spot...

Figure 2: a SEM image of nanowire pair 3-4. b Spatially resolved lasing spectra of nanowire pair 3-4 for two different positions in the inhomogeneous pump spot. Frequency locking can be observed for position 1. For position 2, the frequency locking breaks down and the nanowires emit spectra with an individual mode spacing that fits their respective cavity length.

Figure 3: a Lasing photoluminescence spectra of nanowires 5 and 6 before pair assembly. b Spatially resolved lasing spectra of nanowire pair 5-6 at position 1 in the pump spot. c SEM image of nanowire pair 5-6.
Figure 3 presents the results obtained for a third nanowire pair (NW 5 and NW 6)
